attributes.stem: A 72-year-old woman is admitted to hospital with increasing shortness of breath on a background of atrial fibrillation, STEMI and pacemaker. The intern is asked to present her chest X-ray on the ward round. The image is displayed on the screen and shows a posteroanterior chest radiograph taken while sitting upright, with bat wing opacities, interstitial oedema and pulmonary congestion.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial step in assessing this investigation?
options: Check patient identifiers and technical adequacy
Report on the presence of medical devices
attributes.explanation:
When presenting any radiological image, it is essential to follow a systematic approach to avoid missing important findings. The first step should always be to confirm patient identifiers and technical adequacy. This includes checking the patient's name, date of birth, date of imaging, type of film and ensuring the image is of adequate quality (proper penetration, rotation, and inspiration).

While assessment of the cardiac silhouette is important, it should not be the first step. The cardiac size and contour should be evaluated after confirming image adequacy and examining the mediastinum.

Examination of the lung fields is a crucial component of chest x-ray interpretation, but it should follow a structured approach beginning with image adequacy. The lung fields should be systematically examined for infiltrates, masses, and other abnormalities after the initial steps.

While stating the diagnosis is the ultimate goal of radiological interpretation, it should only be done after a systematic review of all findings. Making a diagnosis before thoroughly examining the image may lead to premature closure and missed findings.

Noting medical devices such as central lines, endotracheal tubes, and chest drains and their position is important, but this comes later in the systematic approach after confirming image adequacy.

A systematic approach to chest x-ray interpretation typically follows this order: patient identifiers and technical adequacy (lung volume, artifacts, penetration, positioning), then soft tissues (including breast shadows), bones, heart and mediastinum, (cardiomegaly, calcification), hila, lungs (divided into zones), pleura, and finally any medical devices. Life in the Fast Line describes this as 'DRSABCDE of the CXR Interpretation' - Details, Ripe (technical quality), Soft Tissue/Bones, Airway, Breathing (lung fields/pleura), Circulation, Diaphragm, and Extras. Either structured method ensures nothing is missed and presentations are clear and professional.
learningPoints: A systematic approach to chest x-ray presentation includes technical quality, soft tissues, bones, heart, mediastinum, lungs, and pleura.
Chest x-rays should be reviewed in a consistent order to avoid missing important findings.
When presenting imaging, start with patient identifiers and technical adequacy before describing findings.
references: Klein J, Rosado-de-Christenson M. Chapter 1: A Systematic Approach to Chest Radiographic Analysis.  Diseases of the Chest, Breast, Heart and Vessels 2019-2022: Diagnostic and Interventional Imaging [Internet].  Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553874/
Skniner S. Guide to thoracic imaging. Australian Family Physician. 2015: 44 (8).  Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2015/august/guide-to-thoracic-imaging
Brims F. DRSABCDE of CXR interpretation. Life in the Fast Line. 2023.  Available at: https://litfl.com/drsabcde-of-cxr-interpretation/
